extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a lab‑scale gantry crane inspired by the harbor cranes of the Port of Antwerp. It is a small cyber‑physical system that transports lightweight pucks representing containers between defined locations. The hardware consists of a mechanical crane structure, a hoist motor driven by a stepper motor and controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller (see Fig. 4), and associated sensors and actuators. The crane is operated via a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface that connects to the motor controllers, a Control Application that sends motion commands, a Logging Application that records state data, and a Human‑Machine Interface (HMI) for operator interaction. The system operates in a laboratory environment and is deliberately designed to be simple yet representative of real‑world container handling, making it suitable for research on synchronization, fault detection, and continuous validation in digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides several tightly coupled services: (1) a Trajectory Generator Service that uses a kinematic model of the crane to compute optimal movement trajectories that eliminate residual swing at the end of motion; (2) a Simulation Service that emulates the crane and its controller dynamics and can run the generated trajectories to predict behaviour; (3) a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares measured trajectories from the physical crane with simulated ones, computes distance metrics, checks them against a configurable threshold and raises alerts via the dashboard; (4) a Dashboard Service implemented with Grafana to visualize time‑series data, simulated confidence bounds and validation alerts; (5) a Database Service based on TimescaleDB that stores all logged state, trajectory and validation data; (6) an MQTT Broker that mediates signaling between the above services. These services support monitoring, optimisation of motion, predictive simulation, validation and visualisation for both operators and researchers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on open‑source, freely available tools: the control and logging logic are written in Python; containerisation and deployment are handled with Docker Compose (see Fig. 7); persistent storage uses a TimescaleDB time‑series database; visualisation uses a Grafana dashboard; inter‑service communication uses an MQTT broker; the architecture is described with the C4 Model; the repository and all source code are hosted publicly on GitHub. The kinematic and simulation models are incorporated into the Trajectory Generator and Simulation Services, respectively, and the Continuous Validation Service uses custom distance‑metric calculations. All components run on a personal computer or a small lab workstation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Data from the physical crane is streamed continuously by the Logging Application into the TimescaleDB; the Continuous Validation Service consumes these measurements in real time and compares them with the output of the Simulation Service. The twin therefore operates in a near‑real‑time (continuous) loop, but the documents do not specify explicit sampling periods, latency budgets, or temporal granularity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The exemplar implements a single digital‑twin instance for the lab‑scale crane. The twin is realized as six tightly coupled containers (Database, Dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, MQTT Broker) that together provide the twin’s functionality. No hierarchical or decentralized multiple‑twin arrangement is described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a micro‑service architecture orchestrated with Docker Compose. The Physical Crane Interface and the five Python applications (Control, Logging, HMI) run as separate processes. Twin services communicate via an MQTT broker, store data in a TimescaleDB instance, and present it through a Grafana dashboard. The overall coordination is depicted with the C4 model and relies on containerization for deployment and resource isolation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is limited to standard protocols: MQTT is used for inter‑service signaling, TimescaleDB provides an SQL‑compatible storage layer, and Grafana accesses the database over HTTP/HTTPS for visualization. No explicit connections to other enterprise systems, external digital twins, or interoperability standards such as AutomationML are mentioned for this exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin comprises a geometric CAD representation of the gantry crane together with an analytical kinematic model that describes the motion of the hoist and trolley. The kinematic model is used by the Trajectory Generator Service to compute optimal swing‑free movement trajectories. A Simulation Service hosts a dynamic simulation of the crane and its low‑level controllers, allowing virtual execution of generated trajectories. All runtime data (position, velocity, motor currents, etc.) are streamed from the Physical Crane Interface to a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The database is accessed by a Grafana dashboard for visualization. Model and service coordination is mediated by an MQTT broker. The twin therefore contains three main model types (geometric CAD, analytical kinematic, and dynamic simulation) and a data pipeline from the physical system to TimescaleDB.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuation hardware consists of a stepper motor that drives the hoist, controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller board. Actuation commands are issued by a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface which communicates with the controller over the controller’s native interface (e.g., USB/serial). The Control Application, also written in Python, sends motion commands (position set‑points, speed profiles) to the interface. Remote operation is provided through the Human Machine Interface front‑end, which allows the operator to start, stop, home and zero the crane. No explicit actuation range or safety limits are documented.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares the measured execution of a trajectory (obtained from the Logging Application) with the corresponding simulated trajectory produced by the Simulation Service. The service computes distance metrics (e.g., positional error) and checks them against a predefined acceptable threshold; violations trigger operator notifications via the Grafana dashboard. The validation pipeline thus provides on‑line verification of the kinematic and dynamic models. The exemplar has been used in research on continuous validation, fault localisation via time‑series classification, and model‑based evolution, indicating that uncertainty is monitored through thresholds and that errors are detected by deviation analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor measurements from the swing‑angle encoder, anemometer and other crane state variables are collected by an Arduino and made available through the Physical Crane Interface, a Python program that connects to the crane controllers. The Logging Application continuously records the crane's state into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. Service components (e.g., Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) receive data via the MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) that is used for signaling between services. The data flow is therefore: physical sensors → Arduino → Python interface → Logging Application → TimescaleDB, with MQTT used for inter‑service communication. No explicit sampling frequency, data‑quality checks, or preprocessing steps are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application, implemented in Python, uses the Physical Crane Interface to issue control commands to the crane controllers based on trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator Service or decisions from other digital‑twin services. Commands are sent as actuator instructions (e.g., motor speed/position set‑points) over the same Python‑based interface used for data acquisition. The documents do not detail command message formats, authentication, validation, feedback confirmation, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All digital‑twin services are containerized using Docker and orchestrated with Docker‑compose. The TimescaleDB database, Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker, and Grafana dashboard run in separate Docker containers on the same host computer and communicate over the Docker network via standard TCP/IP. The Python‑based Physical Crane Interface and control/logging applications run as processes on the host and interact with the containers through the MQTT broker (for signaling) and direct PostgreSQL connections to TimescaleDB. No explicit bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols beyond the use of MQTT and container isolation are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The exemplar is deployed on‑premise on a single personal computer. Docker is used to host the TimescaleDB, MQTT broker, and Grafana components, while the Python modules (gantrylib) are installed via pip. Docker‑compose provides one‑command startup/shutdown of the containers. The code and documentation are publicly available on GitHub and hosted with MkDocs. No cloud or edge platforms are mentioned, and scalability is limited to the resources of the host machine.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The gantry‑crane digital twin follows a development‑to‑operation lifecycle. In the development phase CAD models and a kinematic model of the crane are created and packaged together with Python‑based services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) as Docker images. The architecture is described with the C4 model and all artifacts (source code, Dockerfiles, configuration) are stored in a public GitHub repository. Deployment is performed with a single Docker‑Compose command, which starts the physical‑crane interface, control and logging applications, the MQTT broker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, and the twin services. In the operation phase the physical crane is driven by the Control Application while the Logging Application streams state to the TimescaleDB. The Simulation Service reproduces the commanded trajectory, the Trajectory Generator derives optimal swing‑free trajectories, and the Continuous Validation Service continuously compares measured trajectories with simulated ones, computes distance metrics and checks them against a predefined threshold. When the threshold is exceeded, the operator is notified via the Grafana dashboard, thereby closing the loop between the physical and virtual representations. Lifecycle governance is implicitly enforced by the Docker‑Compose orchestration which guarantees that the same container versions and configurations are used throughout development, testing and runtime.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process is model‑driven and container‑based. CAD and kinematic models are first defined, then encapsulated in Python micro‑services that implement trajectory generation, simulation, and continuous validation. The overall system architecture is documented with the C4 model (physical crane, interfaces, control, logging, HMI, and six digital‑twin containers). All source code, Dockerfiles and configuration files are hosted on a public GitHub repository, providing version control and reproducibility. Quality assurance is realized at runtime by the Continuous Validation Service, which automatically validates each executed trajectory by comparing live sensor data with simulated results, computing a distance metric and checking it against an acceptable threshold. Deployment and repeatable testing are automated with Docker‑Compose (Fig. 7), ensuring that the same container versions are deployed across experiments. Evolution of the twin is supported by the loosely‑coupled MQTT broker that enables new services to be added or existing ones to be updated without breaking existing communications, and by the open‑source nature of the code base that allows researchers to extend or replace individual services.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The digital twin provides decision support through several services. The Trajectory Generator Service uses the kinematic model to compute optimal, swing‑free movement trajectories for the crane, which are then executed by the Control Application. The Continuous Validation Service continuously ingests measured trajectory data from the physical crane, runs the Simulation Service on the same command, and calculates a distance metric between measured and simulated trajectories. If the metric exceeds a configurable threshold, an alert is sent to the operator via the Grafana dashboard and the Human‑Machine Interface, enabling timely corrective actions. The Grafana Dashboard visualizes historical and real‑time data from the TimescaleDB, showing trajectory traces, confidence bounds from the simulator, and system status, allowing operators to monitor performance and make informed operational decisions. The HMI additionally lets users configure logging, initiate homing or zeroing procedures, and adjust controller parameters, thereby closing the loop between insight generation and operator action.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and
**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.


Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database,
the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i)
it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL
queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which
is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the
choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.
Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source
Eclipse Mosquitto was used.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
